questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a CSS comment?
questions.explanation: CSS comments use the /* */ syntax. Double slashes (//) are not valid in CSS, and <!-- --> is for HTML comments.

questions.question: Can CSS comments span multiple lines?
choices: No, each line needs its own comment markers
Yes, everything between /* and */ is commented out
Only if each line starts with an asterisk (*)
Only single-line comments are supported in CSS
questions.explanation: CSS comments can span multiple lines. Everything between the opening /* and closing */ is treated as a comment, regardless of how many lines it covers.

questions.question: What happens when you wrap a CSS rule inside /* and */?
choices: The rule is executed with lower priority
The rule is highlighted in the browser inspector
The rule is ignored by the browser
The rule causes a syntax error
questions.explanation: Wrapping CSS code inside /* */ comments it out, causing the browser to completely ignore those rules. This is a common debugging technique.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid CSS comment?
questions.explanation: CSS does not support single-line comments with //. Only the /* */ syntax is valid for comments in CSS.

questions.question: A CSS comment begins with /* and ends with ___.
questions.explanation: CSS comments are enclosed between /* (opening) and */ (closing). Everything in between is ignored by the browser.

questions.question: Temporarily disabling a CSS rule by wrapping it in /* */ is called ___ out code.
questions.explanation: Commenting out code is a common debugging technique where you wrap code in comment markers to disable it without deleting it.
